content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I'm looking for movies where the main character is losing their mind

**Score:** 53
**Body:** I love movies like Perfect Blue, Black Swan, and American Psycho because of how much they immerse the viewer into the protagonist's skewed worldview. Anything like these I should watch?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Try The Machinist (2004) the main lead is played by Christian Bale. The movie does a fantastic job of forcing you to see the world through his deeply fractured, emaciated perspective.
2. falling down   
with michael douglas
3. In the mouth of madness 
4. 23 with Jim Carey.
5. A Beautiful Mind
6. Pi - 1998

* A paranoid mathematician searches for a key number that will unlock the universal patterns found in nature.
7. Being John Malkovich 
8. Birdman (2014)

Also the cast is stacked.

Michael Keaton 

Zach Galifianakis 

Edward Norton

 Andrea Riseborough 

Amy Ryan 

Emma Stone 

Naomi Watts
9. The Lighthouse

The Shining

Shutter Island

Possibly also - Taxi Driver
10. THE FATHER with Anthony Hopkins.  Dementia rather than mental illness but wonderful and devastating.

BEAU IS AFRAID has Richard Kind as a man with extreme agoraphobia.  The view of the world and its dangers through his eyes is amazing.

## 2. Fun adventure movies like Pirates of the Caribbean/ The Mummy

**Score:** 40
**Body:** I’m rewatching the first three Pirates of the Caribbean movies and I just love the fun tone and scale. The characters are so specific and the stories move so fast and are so engaging, and yet the emotional moments (between Will and his dad) and the grand themes (Jack’s fear of death, destiny etc) are pretty well executed without being overly dark and heavy handed. are there any other movies that balance fun with scale?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Indiana Jones
2. Princess bride 
3. Hook
4. National Treasure
5. The Mask of Zorro!
6. Count of monte cristo
7. Three musketeers with Sutherland and Sheen
8. The Rocketeer, The Mask of Zorro (same writers as the first thee Pirates movies)
9. Not quite as good but Sahara is a fun movie
10. Treasure Planet (2002)

Atlantis: The Lost Empire (2001)

The Lord of the Rings (2001, 2002, 2003)

## 3. Underrated movie: Don Juan DeMarco (1994)

**Score:** 37
**Body:** Very enjoyable movie. Great performances from Johnny Depp and Marlon Brando. The soundtrack is also 🔥 

I don't see this movie mentioned much, but its very good. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. And it has a cameo by the late Selena :,,( She dreamt of making a name for herself in the English film and music industry - this was to be the first of many film appearances for her.

Beautiful score as well.
2. I remember it as a great date movie junior year of HS :)
3. I think people don’t really understand what this film is about. It’s an absolutely lovely film!
  - It's very charming and endearing. Also manages to be unique. 
4. It’s a great movie 
5. A similar jaunt is Brando in The Freshman with Matthew Broderick.  Brando parodies himself in The Godfather. 
6. Funny, i was listening to a podcast yesterday that mentioned this movie.  I hadn’t heard about it since it came out.   Are other people here listening to “What Went Wrong”?
7. I saw it when it was first released, and loved it .  I forced my 28 year old daughter to watch it with me recently and she also fell in love with it. !! 
8. Great one.
9. I love this film!

## 4. Movie recommendations that aren’t incredibly popular or hyped up, any genre?

**Score:** 36
**Body:** I have come into a bit of a movie drought lately, as I have watched most of the really high rated movies everyone says to watch. I am looking for some recommendations to watch without looking up anything about them, just off of their titles and maybe a small description (the genre mainly). I do not mind what genre, I would happily watch any.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’d recommend Coherence, The Vast of Night, Upgrade, The Peanut Butter Falcon, and Take Shelter. They all flew under the radar compared to bigger releases, but I enjoyed every one of them.
2. Flesh + Blood 

Spetters

An American Tale: Fievel Goes West 

Ernest Goes to Camp 

Showgirls 
  - I love Showgirls
3. Adaptation (2002)
4. Lucky Number Slevin

It's a gangster movie with a lot of dark humor, a little bit of action and thriller elements and a very good cast. Josh Hartnett, Bruce Willis, Lucy Liu, Ben Kingsley and Morgan Freeman
5. Niagara Niagara (1997) Ever wonder what the kid from E.T. got up to? Robin Tunney alone makes it worth it. Guy meets girl with tourettes and they road trip to Canada.

The Ice Storm (1997) Great 70s period piece with Christina Ricci

The Hotel New Hampshire (1984) based on the John Irving book. This one is a bit absurdist where a free spirited family runs a hotel. Has a very young Seth Green in the beginning.
6. Josie and The Pussycats (Trust me) - comedy

Or

The Last Boyscout - buddy action comedy
7. Serial Mom. Its a John Waters film and its hilarious ! Its about a suburban housewife who suddenly becomes a serial killer because people are rude. 
8. Bernie starring Jack Black

It's A Disaster (not to be confused with Disaster Movie), It's a slow burn and you've got to be able to enjoy a very dialogue based movie that could be a play as in it all takes place in a house and I would go in as blind as you are willing

Cracks

Freeway from the '90s starring Reese Witherspoon
  - Bernie is amazing. Have you seen Polka King? Another true story and Jack Black does an amazing job keeping the story true. 

## 5. Just watched: Searching (2018) for the first time OMG

**Score:** 18
**Body:** How is this movie not talked about more? Every time I thought I'd figured it out, it completely pulled the rug out from under me. The screenlife format was way better than I expected, and the pacing was ridiculously good.Definitely one of the most entertaining thrillers I've watched in a long time.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's so good!  Missing is great too!
2. dude, same. caught it at a late night showing and the whole theater was dead silent except for gasps. i remember pausing it a few times just to catch my breath, and the ending twist made me rewind. the screenlife thing could've been a gimmick but it's actually what makes the plot work, all those little clues in the background like the open tabs and email drafts are doing so much storytelling. the dad's desktop history montage at the start is a gut punch, sets up the whole emotional hook and it hits hard. john cho's performance is underrated, the way he conveys panic and desperation through a webcam is nuts. his face when he realizes something, you can feel it through the screen. you should check out 'missing' next, it's a standalone sequel but same format and just as tight. glad you found it.
3. Not sure. We loved it.
4. When this came out I went to see it at the local drive-in, unfortunately it was the third movie of the night and I ended up sleeping through it.
5. Cant remember the specifics but I remember enjoying it as a thriller fanatic..
There's also Missing, not a sequel tho
6. Amazing film! In my top 10 for sure, I don’t hear many people mention it which is criminal!
7. LifeHack (2025) is a another recent Screenlife movie that I thought was really good, a little less thriller and more of a heist movie but still has thrills.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Armie Hammer was 'in tears' after seeing his new film Citizen Vigilante in full, didn't realise it would be so 'hateful'

**Score:** 3279

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. > Hammer himself touched upon this in a recent interview with The Hollywood Reporter, saying that he only received a 50-page script after being approached to star in Citizen Vigilante.  

>"I was like, ‘Where’s the rest?’” he said, before impersonating Boll’s response: “‘No, no, no! Ve just go and shoot and have fun. Ve vant you to be great!’” 

>He also said in the same interview that he was so desperate to get back into acting, he “would have done a f***ing cat food commercial.” 


>Even so, the Hammer insider told Belloni that he was horrified by the movie. “I think he knew it certainly leaned toward the right, but Uwe works in a very frantic way,” they continued. 

>“It’s not like he sent him a hundred-page script. When he saw the final product, he was, ‘That was not the movie I thought we made,’ and he freaked the f**k out.”


>They also said Hammer was paid approximately $250,000 for the role. Despite being unhappy about the final results, the source indicated that Hammer would reprise his role for the sequel – if the price is right. 


I was kind of getting his point, but that that last part. This is just bait to get more people to go and watch.
  - 'I fucking hate it and yes I'll do the sequel.'
    - To be fair he knows he won’t ever get another gig again with any credible director or studio lol Kind of like Johnathan Majors doing a movie for Ben Shapiro’s production company 
      - Has he checked if Woody is available?
        - Look, can we get back to talking about Rampart?
2. My brother in Christ you were there.
  - Read the script, acted the scenes… I doubt the film is only hateful in the scenes he is not in.
3. "I had no idea this would be so hateful. I cried when i saw it. 

But if the pile of money is big enough I'll do a sequel."

~Heir to a fortune so large that he could live in the lap of luxury without ever working a day the rest of his life. 
4. I adored him in Man from U.N.C.L.E but then he had to go and be a cannibal or something. 
5. Citizen Vigilante is an Uwe Boll film. That's all you need to know about it. Its going to be amazingly bad.

## 2. Henry Cavill has played some of the most iconic characters across different genres. The circumstances may not have always favored him, but he gave his absolute best in every role.

**Score:** 426
**Body:** Which of his characters is your favorite?


I know he only has a small cameo as Wolverine, but I added it to show that he's played some of the most iconic characters.

He also did great work in The Man from U.N.C.L.E. and Mission: Impossible, where we got to see more of his acting range.

But I still wanted to include Wolverine because of how iconic that character is.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I loved him in the Witcher. 
  - I just didn't love the show. The Witcher
    - The first season had potential but yeah hiring writers who spurn the source material doesn't seem to work out
2. Ministry of Ungentlemanly Warfare.

Such a blast of a movie.
  - I actually really liked this movie. Total bollocks but it was entertaining. This is what movies are for. Escapism.
3. Napoleon Solo in the Man from Uncle. It’s the closest we’ll ever get to seeing a “bond” portrayal for Henry. 
  - He was fantastic in that role.

https://preview.redd.it/b43ksmecltbh1.jpeg?width=1920&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=327fa9cfa3f67460f8767f782ef2712c33eeaa04
  - I really wanted a sequel to Man From UNCLE. It's fun to think about what they could have done with that cast when they didn't have spend so much time on world building.
4. Didn’t he have like 5 seconds of screen time in that Deadpool movie?  He Owned the Mission Impossible movie he was in. 
5. This is the highlight of his career. It's the coolest thing I've ever seen anyone do.

![gif](giphy|30i758wGv1D2Gj7m0i)

## 3. Is Florence Pugh the most profitable actress of the new generation?

**Score:** 370
**Body:** I don't know if it's simply that she has that "star power," but I looked at her filmography and she has practically no commercial flops—just one film, \*Good Person\*, out of about twenty. It really made me wonder why she isn't talked about as the true new star of cinema.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This feels like a marketing post from a 3 month old bot account. 
2. No, we can solve this by looking at total box office amounts. 

She’s a couple billion behind Zendaya. And one of Pugh’s most profitable films is one where Zendaya was the female lead in with Pugh only playing a small role(Dune 2). 

It could change, Pugh is a great actress. But Zendaya is very much a household name now and has been for several years, across multiple generations of audiences. Pugh’s got a bit of a hill to climb here. 
3. Have you heard of Zendeya?
  - This year about to make her insane money 
    - Her and Tom. The Odyssey and Spider-Man within a couple weeks. They are set for life after this summer.
      - And by winter, Dune 3 is feeding generations 
4. Noooo. Zendaya and Emma Stone are.
  - Emma Stone, from the 2007 film, Superbad?
5. No
6. Nah Zendaya is. She pretty much prints money and its why she's cast in everything. I love Florence and she's one of my favourite new actresses but Zendaya is a real Hollywood movie star.

## 4. Which actor who usually plays the protagonist gave an incredible performance as the antagonist? Denzel in Training Day immediately comes to mind

**Score:** 332

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tom Cruise in Collateral. 
  - This. One of his very best movies too. He was so damned good in it. "Yo, homey! Is that my briefcase?" Have to rewatch this scene three or four times before we can continue on with the movie. Every viewing is like that too. :)
2. Robin Williams in Insomnia. He was unsettling in that role. Also his performance in One Hour Photo.
3. kiefer sutherland in phonebooth

his sinister voice carrying the movie was quite an achievement
4. Henry Fonda as Frank in Once Upon a Time in the West

You had the nicest leading man in Hollywood play one of the most sadistic villain of all time. 
5. ![gif](giphy|jq4t2bQP9brOw)
6. Heath
7. Dustin Hoffman in Hook

![gif](giphy|xT9IgJQ9vsEXB371wA)
8. I just rewatched Hanks in Road to Perdition.  Not necessarily a villain, but a mass murderer
9. Patrick Swayze in Point Break.

![gif](giphy|DLCaPFDLyQ848)

## 5. Christopher Nolan defends taking 'creative liberties' in The Odyssey, says it's for those 'who know nothing about Homer'

**Score:** 324

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. How out of touch can you be most people have seen the simpsons 
  - ![gif](giphy|l0HluN8PywCl6Hckg)
  - lmao fair point, half the population probably thinks the cyclops episode is the primary source material at this point
  - Am I so out of touch? No, it’s Christopher Nolan, who is wrong.
2. Hindustan Times has always been my favourite Hollywood film source 
  - You can always trust the Hindustan Times to deliver the truth in a timely manner
3. Christopher Nolan if this tanks:
"DOH!"

WOW. I said "IF". In no way do I expect this movie to tank. I know Nolan has incredible clout and expect this movie to do very well. 

I will say though, I personally have not been impressed with Nolan since Inception. Although they were not bad movies (TDKR excluded. I was not happy with that movie), I just was not impressed with most of his movies. I have not seen Tenet or Oppenheimer yet, but everything else has been MEHH!
  - Nolan made a 3 hour biopic about the man who developed the nuclear bomb and it made almost 1 billion

This is probably making at least 600m.
4. Clickbait-y headline. The full quote makes it clear he's making it for those who do know the text, too.
5. The full quote is: For me, we're making the film for people who know nothing about Homer and this epic poem, and we make it for people who are very interested in that world and love it. You have to make it work for everybody.

So not only for those who know nothing.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. What's the most controversial movie you've ever seen?

**Score:** 27
**Body:** *I recently learned about the backlash surrounding* **The Last Temptation of Christ** *and it made me realize that sometimes the controversy around a movie becomes bigger than the movie itself. I'm not necessarily looking for the most disturbing film, but for movies that sparked protests, censorship, political debates, religious outrage, or even international incidents. Which film controversy do you think was the biggest, and what actually happened?*

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Kids (1995) is one that comes to mind. It's a day-in-the-life look into a group of latchkey teens who are into sex and drugs. I was a teen at the time of release and just couldn't understand the controversy. Some kids lived like that. The film wasn't endorsing their choices, in fact it showed some of the terrifying consequences of less than optimal choices. This was a group of kids in trouble, or heading for it. It seemed more of a prescient social commentary. 

Now that I'm in my 50s I maybe get the controversy a bit more. I remember the film shows underage kids having sex and talking about it explicitly. It's done in a documentary tone, not pornographic, but still I don't want to see that in film. But maybe that's one of the the points?
2. Medium Cool. Late 1960's. Ruined the director's career. At one point they were filming a scene (1968 Chicago during the convention) and an actual riot broke out and they filmed through it.

Hard to find, worth a viewing. 
  - Didn't expect to find this one here, lol. Most other films that are mentioned in this thread are about sex, drugs and death, not politics and activism. It's one of my favorites, a brave and interesting movie.

I didn't know that Medium Cool had such a negative effect on Haskell Wexler's directing career. Although it explains the following big gap in his CV.
3. I think, probably Cannibal Holocaust haha. And it's controversial for good reason too.

Technically, *Life of Brian* was banned by the Catholic Church when it was released - or at least spoke down on by the Pope at the time (I think).

I think the movie *Grotesque* (Japan, 2009) was banned in several countries. That's an... interesting one. That's a one-and-done movie for me haha.
  - My grandfather was arrested for showing Life of Brian in the Sault Ste Marie theatre he managed. The charges were later dropped but I still have the article somewhere. 
4. I believe the 1974 film, "Sweet Movie" generated a lot of controversy and was banned in numerous countries. And I'm selecting it also because normally my reaction to controversial films is one in which I enjoy it or feel it was an overreaction (like, a modern audience wouldn't care). However, Sweet Movie disgusted me even 40yrs later.
  - Very controversial and starting to become obscure because it’s only seldomly talked about and it’s even rarely seen.
5. I took a class on beats and hippies in college. We watched Easy Rider. 

The professor had us sign a waiver at the beginning of class because of the content in the books and movie. 

The thing about Easy Rider for me is that it's so influential in shaping post 1970 Hollywood that didn't seem like a shocking film at all.
  - When was this?
6. Probably Ken Russell's *The Devils* from 1971. Heavily cut before release and still rated X, it received widespread outrage due to its combination of sex, violence, and the church. All this despite being based on true events and being relatively historically accurate.

Unlike The Exorcist which has similar content from a similar time, The Devils takes a negative view of organized religion.

The complete version, including the infamous "Rape of Christ" sequence, has been de facto banned worldwide for over 50 years due to Warner's refusal to release it. I had to watch it in poor secondhand quality on a shady website.

Exciting news is that Warner's has finally capitulated and the full version of this masterpiece has been restored, with a screening at Cannes this year and becoming widely available in the fall. Check it out!

## 2. Gena Rowlands' wall-banging "possession" in Opening Night (1977) - the concept of possession and female transcendence

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Was rewatching the incredible performance of Gena Rowlands in Cassavetes Opening Night, a movie of great force telling of an aging star of the theater who absolutely refuses to act the script of a play in which a woman loses her social/sexual/romantic power because of how society positions women, a decline which is not only inevitable but also regarded as due. The writer of the play is (symbolically) her older self, and >!a 17 year old fan dies early in the film!<, symbolizes her younger self. She is caught between the two, and the film is about all the methods and "weapons" she calls to her aid to avoid becoming what society is telling her she is becoming (both in life, but also as it is scripted in the play). She collapses in rehearsals refusing to be slapped, she becomes erratic and "diva" like. She pleads with the director and argues with the writer. She drinks. She goes to spiritualists, because she is being haunted and in fact abused by the ghost of her younger self. It is a spectacular film and her (2nd) best performance. She is beautiful and full of force. She projects the majesty that she is appealing to as a character and human being.

What is interesting about the film is that in the later scenes, just before opening night (titular), >!when she is so falling down drunk!<, but full of so much fortitude, the scenes read like life and death survival, as a woman. She is desperate, exhausted, out of control, and summoning every fiber of her being to "show up" for the performance that is expected of her. My wife says to me: her performance here is so different in tone, but at a deeper level also quite in sympathy with Isabelle Adjani's subway scene in Possession (1981), a very famous scene which is probably the most out of this world female physical/psychic performance ever captured on film. It struck me immediately as absolutely true. I never had connected the two scenes, some of my favorite in cinema.

And then I reflected that there is an earlier scene in Opening Night where Gena Rowlands is *actually* possessed, and is throwing herself against walls (being beat up by her younger self), creating even greater sympathy between various scenes. It really does read as if these scenes were a few years harbinger inspirations to what Zulawasky would being Isabelle Adjani to do. [I edited them in parallel just to show, in case anyone is interested](https://bsky.app/profile/medias-res.bsky.social/post/3mq2cscftqc22).

One of the curious elements in parallel is that while Opening Night is about a (childless) woman who is reaching a point of the (possible, scripted) demise of her sexual, romantic power, and Possession seems to be telling a different story, that of a woman who brimming with so much adulterous desire and potency, she rebels against the "script" of suburban domesticity (the faithful wife, mother) enough to generate her own demon-lover (out of her own physical miscarriage, said to be the miscarriage of "Faith"). If I recall, it has been reported that Adjani who was about 20 at the time, refused the role originally because she did NOT want to play a mother (I believe she actually had a child somewhat in secret then). Once she played a mother she would be marked as **not** the "ingenue" by the industry, at the time one of the most alluring young actresses in France. She felt exactly the pressure that Gena Rowlands' character did. If she played "mother" (or Gena an aging woman) well, one would be forever slotted. Both characters reach an absolute breaking point of "possession", a spiritual charge that goes beyond all rationality, in order to transcend, break free from society roles, the script given to them.

Female possession and quasi-possession holds a very long history in indicating this possibility of freedom, from the possession of witches and their alternate power lore (outside the morality of Christendom), to other, imbued monstrous female depictions. It is interesting as well that Maggie Gyllenhaal's recent The Bride! (2026) also centered on possession, as the Mary-Shelly-possessed The Bride cycles through variously scripted roles that are stereotypically given to a supposedly "liberated" woman, seeking to break free in her new Frankensteined Self. In cinema history this trope of female (transgressive, unnatural) possession is there at the root of the monster/femme fatale figure, reaching back to silent film and early sound horror: German silent film Alraune (The Mandrake, 1928, w/ Brigitte Helm), Dracula's Daughter (1936, w/ Gloria Holden) and Cat People (1942, w/ Simone Simon).

These two wall-banging scenes in particular, not far from each other in time, reached new heights in the desperate female portrayal, beating themselves against social confines & opportunity. Notably, both films, Opening Night and Possession, are about female **artists** (Possession is the tale of his divorce from his actress wife), fashioning liberation out of the bounds of their own body/psyche, using the powers of artistic possession itself.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Oh, so [Young Fathers and Massive Attack](https://youtu.be/A3XDkgEgAkg) were referencing *Posssession* 😧 mind blown

Oh, so [Young Fathers and Massive Attack](https://youtu.be/A3XDkgEgAkg) were referencing *Posssession* 😧 mind blown

Oh, so [Young Fathers and Massive Attack](https://youtu.be/A3XDkgEgAkg) were referencing *Posssession* 😧 mind blown

Oh, so [Young Fathers and Massive Attack](https://youtu.be/A3XDkgEgAkg) were referencing *Posssession* 😧 mind blown

Oh, so [Young Fathers and Massive Attack](https://youtu.be/A3XDkgEgAkg) were referencing *Posssession* 😧 mind blown

## 3. Does anyone know of any films centering on urban environments and city nightlife, where cinematography or aesthetics is prioritized over plot?

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Sorry for the awkward title, but to explain a bit further, I'm something of a philistine when it comes to cinema, and I'm mostly coming to cinema with a deep engagement with literature as my background. Increasingly my literary interests over the past few years have been drawn towards a kind of formalism or aestheticism - I'm especially drawn towards extended and extensive physical descriptions, and descriptions of cities and the urban nightlife most of all. While these novels aren't plotless a lot of times the plot is subservient to or a vehicle for poetic descriptions or extended investigations of particular settings. I'm wondering if anyone knows of any films that might have a similar objective? One might boil this down to "films where the city is a main character" which is largely true, but perhaps taken to the extreme. The film I've found which best hits these buttons for me is Malick's Knight of Cups. The narrative is rightly criticized for being underbaked, but I've never found a film that better captures that flaneurish experience of wandering dazed through a massive city. Ideally I'd love something else like this with a greater focus on urban streets, or nighttime settings. This might sound super specific but I'm of course open to broad recommendations, but also as a confessed cinema philistine I'd be interested in knowing if there are any styles or schools of cinema more broadly that compare to this kind of descriptive-heavy aesthetic literature I've mentioned. Of course literature and cinema are two distinct mediums, and maybe it's not super obvious how "poetic description" translates to cinema, and also it seems that a general focus on aesthetics and cinematography are one of the defining features of "high cinema" or artistic film from Netflix slop or Hollywood blockbusters, but more specifically I'm wondering about film that is focused primarily on the act of seeing or representing environments rather than telling stories.

Again, sorry if any of this is unclear or rambly, but I'd love to hear what anyone has to say

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Shame (2011)

Good Time (2017)

Naked (1993)

Elevator to the Gallows (1958)

Sult (1966)

Beauty and the Dogs (2017)

Victoria (2015)

After Hours (1986)

Taxi Driver (1976)

Exotica (1994)
  - I would humbly add Drive (2011).  

Also, Michael Mann movies like Collateral, Thief, and Miami Vice really give you a great feeling and view of various parts of their respective locales (i.e., LA, Chicago, and Miami) in artistic ways.
2. I'd recommend looking at Wong Kar-Wai's work, especially stuff like Chungking Express or Fallen Angels. He's telling very character focused stories but the urban landscapes are very much present as more than just background settings in his work, which I think might be of interest to you. Fallen Angels in particular plays with space in interesting ways to explore themes of isolation and intimacy while living in a dense city that I find particularly interesting. 






  - Totally this. For those of us that remember pre-handover Hong Kong, WKW is like a memory fever dream.
3. I think you are looking for the [City Symphony ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/City_symphony) genre. I've watched Man With a Movie Camera, Berlin: Symphony of a Great City, and Manhatta. They vary in terms of content from focusing on urban infrastructure and architecture, to focusing on human interactions and life. Man With a Movie Camera is an classic though, and is still immensely engaging and entertaining.

It's not *exactly* the same, but Koyaanisqatsi fits the bill as well, and is gorgeous. 
  - I would second Koyaanisqatsi. It's what I came here to say. 

It is probably the defining film for the idea of *aesthetics* of the city over a plot. It's beautifully shot, surprisingly gripping, and weirdly unnerving.  
4. My first thought when reading your description was Michelangelo Antonioni's 1962 film, L'Eclisse, or The Eclipse.

In that final montage, the main characters miss their meeting, and the film concludes with about seven or eight minutes of haunting, almost abstract shots of the Roman cityscape, emphasizing the emptiness and alienation of the modern world. 
5. Chungking Expess, Wong Kar Wai

Slacker (also Before I and II), Linklater

Early Vardá like Cléo de 5 a 7

Early Almodóvar like Laberinto de Pasiones

Breathless (also Band à Part, but suburban), Godard

Jules et Jim, Truffaut

Wings of Desire (also Alice in the Cities), Wenders

Air Doll, Kore Eda
6. Already mentioned, but Wong Kar Wai does a great job with urban environments. To my mind Happy Together is especially strong in this regard- the streets of Buenos Aries and then Taipei are a major character in the film (maybe not the main character though). 

The Thai director Weerasethakul will sometimes allow the environment to actually take over the narrative of his films, although he switches from urban to rural and also from night to day. I recall the ending of Syndromes And A Century abandons the characters and narrative completely and focuses on the city streets and people. Memoria features sequences in Bogotá at night where the environment really becomes the main character. Weerasethakul generally would be exactly what you're looking for, except his films tend to fall into a pattern where it's the rural environment that ultimately dominates.
7. Playtime (1967): This is quite literally what you're looking for. It is very light on narrative, and very abstract, edging on a fever dream.

Koyaanosqatsi(1982): This is probably the pinnacle of no plot, maximum visualisation. Others have recommended it already, but I want to reiterate it. There are 2 more films in this series as well if you like it.

## 4. I watched “Boy I’m scared” and it got me questioning mortality.

**Score:** 9
**Body:** I recently watched a seven-minute short film called *Boy I'm Scared*. Three friends spend what may be their final day together, simply talking.  
What surprised me wasn't the fear of death, but how quietly the film approached mortality.

It left me wondering whether accepting mortality is something we ever actually learn.  
The film opens with something small — a tired sigh — that made me feel as though there was something wrong with the world I was just introduced to. It made me think that perhaps accepting death isn't a dramatic realization but something much quieter.  
That led me to another question: if you knew today was your last day, would acceptance even be possible? Or is fear inseparable from being human?  
I'm curious how other people interpreted the film. Did it leave you thinking about mortality, or did something else stand out?

## 5. "Jennifer’s Body", Megan Fox’s Media Body and the Wish That Becomes a Curse in "Obsession"

**Score:** 5
**Body:** Hello everyone!

I wrote [a film-theory essay](https://substack.com/home/post/p-205683078) on Jennifer’s Body and Curry Barker’s Obsession, with Michel Tournier’s Abel Tiffauges as a literary prelude.

Tiffauges helps frame the argument because his cannibalism begins as a way of reading the world. Before the literal mouth opens, the world has already become food for an inner myth. That makes him useful for thinking about films where desire passes through images, bodies, and cultural signs until it becomes difficult to contain.

My reading of Jennifer’s Body centers on Megan Fox’s public image. I approach the film through a distorted version of the political-theological trope of the king’s two bodies: the real person and the symbolic body that belongs to the public gaze. Fox enters the film already carrying a media body made by 2000s Hollywood, a sex-symbol image built to withstand mass attention. Jennifer gives that image a fictional body, then gives it appetite. The film seems to offer a chance to revise the image: to add irony, menace, interiority, and the right to look back. Yet the image absorbs those meanings and preserves itself as something sharper. Jennifer becomes beauty with a maw.

This is also what happens inside the plot. Low Shoulder treat Jennifer’s body as a readable ritual object, as if beauty and supposed virginity could be converted into success. Their mistake is a fantasy of total interpretation. The body they think they understand returns as a predatory image that reads and consumes them instead.

Obsession gives the same problem a more intimate form. Bear’s wish begins as grief and the need to be confirmed by another person’s love. Once the wish is granted, it stops behaving like a feeling. Nikki becomes the body occupied by Bear’s externalized desire, while Bear becomes the object around which that desire arranges the world. The wish turns into a curse because it continues to appear after it has lost any human measure.

The essay is about films where desire receives too much being: where an image becomes strong enough to devour the meanings attached to it, and reality begins to bend around what should have remained fantasy.  
  
Essay: [https://substack.com/home/post/p-205683078](https://substack.com/home/post/p-205683078)
